body: Tyler came home with a TikTok account on his phone. He's 11. I don't know if you set that up or if he did it himself but it needs to come off. We agreed no social media until 13.
He set it up himself. And honestly I'm not sure it's the end of the world. All his friends have it. You can't keep him in a bubble forever, Petra.

So you're okay with him using a platform with zero parental controls and predatory content algorithms? Great parenting, Gregg. We had a clear agreement and you just let it slide because it's easier than actually being a parent.

Don't lecture me about parenting. You're the one who gave him the phone in the first place with no restrictions set up. I checked — there were zero screen time limits when he came to me. So maybe look in the mirror before you start throwing accusations.
The screen time limits were set. He figured out the passcode — which he obviously got from your house because I certainly didn't give it to him. And of course you'd find a way to make this my fault.

I'm not going to sit here and be blamed for everything that goes wrong. You want to delete TikTok? Fine. But then you also need to stop letting him stay up until midnight watching YouTube on YOUR watch, which he told me about last week. Deal with your own house first.

That is completely irrelevant and you know it. This is about TikTok and you deflecting because you have no actual response. I'm deleting the app when he's here. If you reinstate it that's on you and I'll document it.

Document away. And while you're documenting, document the fact that you told him his iPad time at my house 'doesn't count' toward homework. He's completely confused about rules because you keep undermining me. We're going in circles. I'm done with this conversation tonight.
Of course you're done. You're always done when you don't get your way. This is why nothing ever gets resolved. Don't come to me next week confused about why he has no consistent boundaries.
